title: Xenophyllum ciliolatum
intro: Xenophyllum ciliolatum, also known as the Mexican globe mallow, is a plant species belonging to the family Plantaginaceae, characterized by its bright yellow flowers and unique, globe-shaped seed pods. Traditionally, the plant has been used in folk medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, respiratory infections, and inflammation, due to its potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Xenophyllum ciliolatum include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and glycosides, which are believed to be responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, the plant is often used to prepare teas, infusions, and decoctions, as well as to make tinctures and capsules, which are used to treat respiratory issues, such as bronchitis and coughs, and to boost the immune system.
taxonomy: Xenophyllum ciliolatum is classified within the family Plantaginaceae, which is a family of flowering plants commonly known as the plantain family. This family is part of the order Lamiales, which comprises plants with bilaterally symmetrical flowers. Xenophyllum ciliolatum is native to the Americas and is a member of the genus Xenophyllum, which contains about 17 species of plants. The taxonomy of Xenophyllum ciliolatum is subject to ongoing research and revision. As a result, some sources may place it in different classifications, but it is generally agreed to be part of the family Plantaginaceae.
